# **Comprehensive Technical Specification and Tutorial: The Agent2Agent (A2A) and Agent-to-UI (A2UI) Protocols**

## **Introduction to the Protocol-Driven Agent Architecture**

The rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence systems has precipitated a
fundamental paradigm shift in software engineering, moving the industry from
isolated, monolithic models toward highly distributed, multi-agent
architectures. In these decentralized environments, specialized algorithmic
agents—ranging from financial analysts and logical orchestrators to inventory
managers and travel planners—must collaborate continuously to execute complex,
multi-step workflows. Historically, connecting these disparate systems required
custom integration code and bespoke application programming interfaces (APIs),
resulting in brittle infrastructure, significant maintenance overhead, and a
lack of true interoperability.1 The sheer volume of bespoke "glue code" required
to maintain connections between differing proprietary frameworks effectively
stymied the development of a broader, collaborative agent ecosystem.1

To eliminate these technological silos, the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol was
introduced. Originally developed by Google and subsequently donated to the
open-source community under the Linux Foundation, the A2A protocol provides a
universal, standardized communication layer that enables independent,
potentially opaque AI agents to dynamically discover, negotiate, and
collaborate.4 This standardization means that agents can work together without
needing to share their underlying proprietary logic, internal memory structures,
or specific tool stacks.4 Following the integration of IBM's Agent Communication
Protocol (ACP) into the specification, A2A has emerged as the preeminent
industry standard for inter-agent communication, serving as the foundational
public network for artificial intelligence interoperability.2

By treating every AI agent as a network-accessible service governed by a shared
set of interaction rules, A2A facilitates a highly composable ecosystem where
specialized agents act as modular building blocks.5 This standardized layer is
further complemented by the Agent-to-User Interface (A2UI) protocol, a
declarative open standard that allows generative agents to safely construct
interactive user interfaces directly from their outputs, streaming these
interface definitions across the A2A transport layer to front-end client
applications.8

This comprehensive research report serves as an exhaustive technical tutorial on
the mechanics and implementation of the A2A protocol. It provides a meticulous
examination of its JSON-RPC 2.0 messaging formats, the architectural
implementation of Server-Sent Events (SSE) for continuous streaming, the precise
logical mechanisms governing data chunking, and the standardized procedures for
encapsulating and transmitting A2UI interface payloads over the A2A transport
network.

## **Core Architectural Primitives of the A2A Protocol**

The architecture of the A2A protocol relies on a flexible, bidirectional
client-server model tailored specifically for the unique demands of autonomous
and semi-autonomous artificial intelligence operations. Within this paradigm, an
agent can act as an A2A Client, which is responsible for delegating tasks,
managing user inputs, and orchestrating broader workflows.4 Alternatively, an
agent can act as an A2A Server (Remote Agent), which exposes an A2A-compliant
HTTP endpoint designed to receive incoming requests, process specialized tasks,
and return analytical results or status updates.4

Crucially, an individual agent is not locked into a single role; it can
dynamically switch operations, acting as a server when receiving instructions
from a human user or a primary orchestrator, while simultaneously acting as a
client when it needs to delegate sub-tasks to highly specialized peer agents.5
This flexibility allows developers to construct complex topological structures,
ranging from simple hierarchical trees to vast, decentralized operational
meshes.5

Interaction within this decentralized ecosystem is strictly governed by a
sequence of foundational data primitives.

| A2A Primitive  | Primary Function and Characteristics                                                                                                            | Protocol Lifecycle Role                        |
| :------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- |
| **Agent Card** | A machine-readable JSON metadata document advertising the server's identity, skills, endpoints, and authentication requirements.                | Capability Discovery and Network Integration.1 |
| **Task**       | The fundamental, stateful unit of work possessing a unique identifier and transitioning through a defined lifecycle (submitted, working, etc.). | Stateful Execution and Workflow Management.2   |
| **Message**    | The atomic unit of conversational exchange within a task, containing a designated role and housing payload data.                                | Synchronous and Asynchronous Communication.2   |
| **Part**       | The subdivision of a message supporting multimodal content, typed strictly as text, files, or structured data.                                  | Multimodal Content Delivery and Negotiation.7  |
| **Artifact**   | The terminal deliverable or tangible product generated by the server upon the successful completion of a delegated task.                        | Output Aggregation and Final Result Delivery.2 |

The Agent Card serves as the primary mechanism for decentralized capability
discovery. Published at a standardized, well-known endpoint (typically
/.well-known/agent-card.json), this metadata profile functions analogously to an
API specification or a model card for large language models.1 The card details
the agent's name, version, operational modalities, supported A2A features (such
as push notifications or streaming), and specific skill identifiers.1 This
metadata allows orchestrator agents to programmatically query a network, locate
an appropriate peer capable of fulfilling a specialized requirement, and verify
its communication parameters without requiring hardcoded, rigid routing logic.1

Once an appropriate remote agent is discovered, the interaction centers on the
creation and management of a Task. Unlike stateless REST API calls, A2A
interactions are heavily task-oriented to accommodate the inherently
long-running nature of AI reasoning, inference generation, and multi-step tool
execution.2 A task encapsulates the operational goal and context, progressing
through a rigorously defined lifecycle consisting of states such as submitted,
working, input-required, completed, and failed.2 This stateful design is an
absolute necessity for multi-turn conversations and collaborative background
processing, ensuring that clients can effectively monitor progress over extended
temporal periods.7

Messages constitute the conversational turns exchanged within the overarching
context of a task. Each message includes a designated role (such as "user" for
client-sent instructions or "agent" for server-sent replies) and houses the
actual payload data.2 To support the increasingly multimodal nature of
artificial intelligence, messages are structurally subdivided into Parts. The
protocol formally defines specific, typed formats for these components. A
TextPart is utilized for conveying plain text content and natural language
strings.11 A FilePart represents binary data, which can be transmitted either as
inline base64-encoded bytes for smaller assets or referenced via a secure URI
for larger documents, including critical metadata such as filename and media
type.11 Finally, a DataPart carries structured, machine-readable JSON data,
which is essential for transmitting forms, precise parameters, or specialized
interface commands.11

The ultimate output generated by an A2A server upon task completion is
classified as an Artifact. Similar to messages, artifacts consist of one or more
parts, but they explicitly represent the terminal deliverable of the delegated
task, whether that deliverable is a newly generated analytical report, an
aggregated dataset, or an executable script.2 The distinction between transient
conversational messages and the finalized artifact is a core structural feature
that allows clients to cleanly separate ongoing dialogue from the final,
actionable product.

## **The Messaging Format: JSON-RPC 2.0 Integration**

To guarantee maximum interoperability, operational predictability, and ease of
parsing across diverse enterprise technical stacks, the A2A protocol mandates
the strict use of JSON-RPC 2.0 as the standardized payload format for all
requests and responses.4 While contemporary Representational State Transfer
(REST) architectures define interactions around resources and heavily utilize
HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to manipulate those resources, JSON-RPC 2.0
operates on a fundamentally different philosophy.13 It is a stateless,
lightweight remote procedure call protocol strictly oriented around executing
specific operational methods on a remote server, typically via a single, unified
endpoint.13

The strategic decision to utilize JSON-RPC 2.0 reflects the deeply procedural
and action-oriented nature of multi-agent collaboration. In an agentic network,
clients must explicitly instruct servers to perform highly specific,
parameterized actions (e.g., "summarize this document," "cancel that operation,"
or "subscribe to these updates") rather than merely fetching static state
representations.13 To support this, all HTTP requests and responses carrying
these JSON-RPC payloads within the A2A ecosystem must specify a Content-Type
HTTP header strictly set to application/json.4

![][image1]

## **Structural Anatomy of the JSON-RPC Request**

When an A2A client initiates communication with a remote agent, it dispatches an
HTTP POST request containing a standardized, rigorously structured JSON object.
This request object must strictly adhere to the JSON-RPC 2.0 specification,
comprising four essential, highly defined fields.10

The jsonrpc field is an immutable requirement; it must exclusively contain the
string literal "2.0", explicitly declaring the protocol version being utilized
to prevent legacy parsing errors.10

The method field requires a string identifying the specific A2A protocol
operation that the client wishes to invoke on the server. The A2A specification
defines a core suite of standardized operations. The message/send method is
utilized for initiating synchronous interactions or submitting new inputs to an
existing task.10 The message/stream method establishes a continuous, real-time
streaming response.12 Additional administrative methods include tasks/get to
manually poll the state of an ongoing task, tasks/cancel to prematurely
terminate processing, and tasks/pushNotificationConfig/set to configure
asynchronous webhook delivery.10

The params field houses a structured object containing the granular arguments
required by the invoked method. For instance, when a client executes a call to
the message/send method, it must include a params object containing a properly
formatted Message entity. This message entity specifies the role (e.g., "user"),
an immutable messageId, and an array of typed parts defining the actual
multimodal content being submitted for analysis.10

Finally, the id field establishes a unique, client-generated identifier for the
specific request.10 Because A2A inherently supports asynchronous processing,
concurrent operations, and highly complex long-running tasks, the server is
absolutely required to include this identical id in its eventual response.10
This cryptographic tracking enables the client to securely and deterministically
correlate incoming responses with their originating requests, maintaining state
consistency across the distributed network.10

| JSON-RPC 2.0 Request Field | Data Type      | Specification Requirement and A2A Context                                              |
| :------------------------- | :------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| jsonrpc                    | String         | Must always be exactly "2.0".10                                                        |
| method                     | String         | Defines the A2A operation (e.g., message/send, tasks/cancel).10                        |
| params                     | Object/Array   | Contains the requisite payload, such as a Message object detailing the task.10         |
| id                         | String/Integer | A unique client-side tracker required for correlating asynchronous server responses.10 |

## **Structural Anatomy of the JSON-RPC Response**

Upon the successful execution of an invoked method, the A2A server formulates
and returns a corresponding JSON-RPC response object. This response object
perpetually retains the jsonrpc version identifier and carefully mirrors the
correlating id provided in the initial request.10 Instead of a params field, a
successful response includes a result field containing the structured data
output explicitly defined by the invoked method.10

For example, a successful message/send response will populate the result field
with a comprehensive Task object. This returned object provides the client with
the task's initialized state, its unique remote identifier, a generated context
grouping ID, and any immediate artifacts or preliminary messages generated by
the server during the initial processing phase.10

Conversely, if the operation encounters a failure—whether due to malformed
syntax, insufficient permissions, or internal processing errors—the server
completely omits the result field. Instead, it populates an error object.10 The
error object provides a standardized numeric error code. The protocol utilizes
predefined JSON-RPC codes (such as \-32700 for fatal JSON parse errors, or
\-32601 when a client requests a method not found on the server) alongside
custom A2A protocol-specific codes.10 Accompanying the code is a human-readable
message string, and an optional data field containing granular, error-specific
diagnostics designed to aid developer debugging.10

This rigid, highly predictable structural paradigm guarantees that orchestrator
clients can algorithmically handle wildly varying outcomes across a diverse
fleet of remote agents without being forced to implement fragile, custom parsing
logic for each distinct vendor or framework.2 However, security engineers
emphasize that because A2A relies so heavily on JSON serialization, the standard
JSON attack surface heavily applies. Implementations must rigorously sanitize
inputs to defend against Unicode normalization exploits, deeply nested object
depth attacks designed to trigger stack overflows, oversized payload injections,
and dynamic type handling vulnerabilities.2

## **Streaming Transport and Server-Sent Events (SSE)**

While synchronous message/send operations, combined with periodic polling via
tasks/get, are functionally adequate for rapid, atomic tasks, the generative
nature of modern artificial intelligence frequently requires prolonged
processing times. Generating a complex multi-page report, analyzing massive
external datasets, or compiling code can induce computational latency that
easily exceeds standard, rigid HTTP timeout thresholds.12 To address this
fundamental limitation and provide a highly responsive user experience, the A2A
protocol natively supports robust real-time streaming transport utilizing
Server-Sent Events (SSE).4

SSE provides a highly efficient, unidirectional, server-to-client streaming
mechanism operating continuously over a single, persistent HTTP connection.12
The A2A specification actively and intentionally selects SSE over WebSockets for
this primary real-time transport layer. This architectural decision reflects a
strict prioritization of protocol simplicity, robust corporate firewall
traversal, and an acknowledgment of the inherently unidirectional flow of
generative output.12 While WebSockets permit bidirectional framing, they
introduce significant overhead in connection management and load balancing. A2A
prioritizes simplicity by relying on SSE to push continuous task updates and
generative chunks from the server downward, while simultaneously managing all
new client inputs or interruptions through separate, distinct, standard HTTP
POST requests.12

To formally initiate a streaming session, the A2A client invokes the
message/stream method, or the tasks/resubscribe method if it needs to reconnect
to an ongoing background task.4 The remote server validates the request,
verifies its own capabilities, and acknowledges it with an HTTP 200 OK status.
Crucially, this acknowledgment must be accompanied by a Content-Type HTTP header
explicitly set to text/event-stream.4 Following this handshake, the underlying
HTTP connection is deliberately held open by both parties.17

As the remote agent progresses through its complex background processing
routines, it actively pushes incremental updates back over this persistent
connection.17 In strict, uncompromising adherence to the W3C Server-Sent Events
standard, each transmitted update is formatted as an independent SSE event
containing a specific data field.4 The A2A protocol enforces a critical rule
here: the string payload embedded within this data field must be a complete,
fully formed JSON-RPC 2.0 response object.4 This brilliant engineering
requirement ensures structural consistency regardless of the underlying
transport protocol. A client application parsing a continuous SSE stream
utilizes the exact identical JSON-RPC validation and processing logic as a
client processing a synchronous, one-off HTTP POST response.4

To effectively implement this high-concurrency capability, the server-side
architecture requires a robust asynchronous processing foundation.17 Standard
blocking web frameworks are insufficient. Frameworks such as FastAPI or
Starlette in Python are commonly utilized, allowing the main server event loop
to gracefully manage the open SSE connection concurrently while background
worker threads (often utilizing asyncio.create_task models) execute the actual
heavy inference tasks.17 These busy background routines deposit their
progressive outputs into internal asynchronous queues (e.g., asyncio.Queue).17
Specialized generator functions then continuously monitor these queues,
consuming the raw outputs, formatting them into the correct SSE data string
payloads (containing the A2A JSON-RPC response), and yielding them back to the
waiting client.17

Furthermore, production-grade implementations must meticulously handle
unexpected network disconnections. The server code must catch cancellation
errors gracefully, cleaning up computational resources and halting the
generative model if necessary.17 Simultaneously, clients must implement
exponential backoff strategies for reconnection, utilizing the tasks/resubscribe
method to pass the taskId and instruct the server to resume the stream precisely
from the last confirmed state, ensuring no data is lost during transit
disruptions.17

![][image2]

## **Chunking Mechanisms and Task Lifecycle Management**

The A2A protocol distinguishes meticulously between metadata updates regarding
overarching task progression and the actual physical delivery of generative
content.17 Within a persistent streaming session, this crucial distinction is
managed through two primary, distinct event types emitted by the server: the
TaskStatusUpdateEvent and the TaskArtifactUpdateEvent.12

A TaskStatusUpdateEvent is explicitly designed to signal administrative
transitions within the task's overarching lifecycle.12 When the server
successfully commences processing, it emits this event, updating the task status
to working. This payload may optionally include an embedded message containing a
TextPart that provides a human-readable progress indicator to the user, such as
"Analyzing documentation parameters..." or "Querying external database...".17
Crucially, these status updates control a vital boolean flag designated as
final. Throughout the entire duration of the active processing phase, the final
flag must remain explicitly set to false, informing the client that the
operation is ongoing and the connection must be preserved.17

Conversely, to transmit the actual generative output of the agent—which often
represents the rapid, continuous stream of tokens emitted by a Large Language
Model (LLM)—the server utilizes the TaskArtifactUpdateEvent.12 As the remote
agent generates paragraphs of text, lines of code, or complex structured data,
these fragments are pushed down the SSE connection enclosed specifically within
this event type.17 Because vast generative outputs cannot be transmitted
instantaneously as a single block, the protocol provides explicit chunking
fields located within the Artifact object of the payload.10

When a client application receives a TaskArtifactUpdateEvent, it must examine
the Artifact object for the append flag. If append is set to true, the client is
programmatically instructed to concatenate the incoming data chunk directly onto
the end of the previously received artifact data possessing the exact same
artifactId.17 The client continues this ongoing buffering and aggregation
process in real-time as subsequent events arrive. To signal the end of a
specific output stream, the protocol mandates the use of a lastChunk boolean
flag.17 When the generative model completes its output for a specific artifact,
the server ensures the final TaskArtifactUpdateEvent sets lastChunk: true. This
explicit signal tells the client that the specific data stream is complete, the
buffer can be flushed, and the artifact can be finalized and fully rendered
within the user interface.17

| Streaming Event Type        | Primary Purpose                                                       | Key Control Flags                     | Client-Side Action Required                                                                  |
| :-------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **TaskStatusUpdateEvent**   | Communicates administrative state changes (e.g., submitted, working). | final (boolean)                       | Update UI progress indicators; keep connection open if final is false.                       |
| **TaskArtifactUpdateEvent** | Delivers sequential chunks of the generative output or final data.    | append (boolean), lastChunk (boolean) | Concatenate data to local buffer if append is true; finalize rendering if lastChunk is true. |

The entire streaming interaction formally concludes when the server emits one
terminal TaskStatusUpdateEvent.17 This final event shifts the overall
overarching task state to either completed or failed based on the operational
outcome. Critically, it sets the final flag to true, an explicit instruction to
the client that the background process is terminated and the HTTP connection may
now be safely closed.17 This strict, architectural separation of operational
state (status updates) from generative payload output (artifact updates) ensures
that orchestrator agents can monitor job progress independently of the payload
data size. This allows for high-fidelity progress tracking, robust error
recovery, and clean memory management across complex multi-agent workflows.17

## **Asynchronous Task Updates via Push Notifications**

While Server-Sent Events are ideal for tasks that provide continuous generative
output, some agentic workflows require operations that may take hours or even
days to complete, such as training a sub-model or scraping an entire domain. In
these scenarios, maintaining an open HTTP connection is architecturally
unfeasible and resource-intensive.10 For asynchronous, disconnected operations,
the A2A protocol provides robust support for Push Notifications via webhooks.10

To utilize this capability, the client must first establish a secure return
path. The client configures its webhook by sending a request using the
tasks/pushNotificationConfig/set JSON-RPC method.10 This request includes the
specific Task ID and a PushNotificationConfig object detailing the client's
webhook URL and its required authentication schemes.10

When a significant state change occurs within the long-running task—such as
transitioning to completed, failed, canceled, or input-required—the remote A2A
Server initiates an HTTP POST request entirely independent of the original
connection.10 This server-initiated POST request targets the client's configured
webhook URL, carrying the updated Task object as the JSON payload.10 This allows
the client application to sleep or process other tasks entirely, awakening only
when the remote agent proactively signals that the extended operation requires
attention or has delivered its final artifacts.10

## **Introduction to A2UI: The Agent-to-User Interface Protocol**

While the A2A protocol excels at coordinating background tasks, securely
delegating operations, and exchanging raw, machine-readable data, rendering
complex, multimodal outputs directly to human end-users poses a separate,
distinct engineering challenge. Historically, passing rich, interactive user
interfaces across deep trust boundaries relied on executing arbitrary,
agent-generated code directly on the client, or rendering external content
within isolated, highly sandboxed iframes.8 Both legacy approaches introduce
severe consequences: arbitrary code execution presents unacceptable security
vulnerabilities, while iframes result in degraded, non-native user experiences
that break brand consistency and accessibility standards.8

To definitively solve this, the Agent-to-User Interface (A2UI) protocol was
developed as a complementary, open-source declarative standard.8 A2UI is a
declarative UI specification explicitly optimized for the generative patterns of
Large Language Models.8 It allows an AI agent to comprehensively dictate the
structure and data of an interface without ever sending a single line of
executable code.8

The agent transmits a standardized JSON payload detailing its intent and the
desired components—such as specific text fields, data charts, layout columns, or
interactive buttons.8 The receiving client application then maps these generic,
declarative descriptions directly to its own native, tightly controlled,
pre-approved component libraries (e.g., React, Flutter, Angular, or native iOS
SwiftUI).8 This declarative paradigm ensures complete, ironclad security against
UI injection attacks, as the agent can only request components that the client
has explicitly authorized. Simultaneously, it grants the host application total,
unyielding control over styling, accessibility compliance, and overarching brand
consistency, ensuring the agent's output always feels perfectly native to the
host application.8

## **The Adjacency List Component Model**

The architectural cornerstone of the A2UI protocol, and the feature that makes
it uniquely suited for generative AI, is its utilization of the Adjacency List
Model for defining UI component structures, a massive departure from traditional
nested JSON trees.23

In conventional UI representations across web development, child components are
deeply and physically nested within their parent objects in the data structure.
While logical for static parsing by deterministic compilers, deep nesting
presents immense, often insurmountable challenges for generative AI models. LLMs
operate sequentially, emitting tokens one by one, and frequently fail to close
deeply nested brackets accurately in a single pass, resulting in corrupted JSON
payloads.23 Furthermore, deep nesting fundamentally impedes progressive
rendering. A client application utilizing a nested structure must often wait to
receive the entire, fully-closed JSON tree before it can confidently render the
parent container to the user.23

A2UI bypasses these critical limitations entirely by representing the complete
user interface as a flat array of component objects, with all hierarchical
relationships mapped entirely through simple ID references.23 Every single
component instance generated within an A2UI payload is assigned a unique string
identifier (a ComponentId).24 A layout container component, such as a Column or
a Row, rather than nesting its children directly within its JSON object
structure, simply provides an array of string references mapping to the unique
IDs of those children.24

| Architectural Feature           | Traditional Nested JSON                                       | A2UI Adjacency List Model                                             |
| :------------------------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------ | :-------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Data Structure**              | Deeply embedded, cascading child objects                      | Flat, single-level list of distinct component objects                 |
| **Relationship Mapping**        | Implicitly defined via physical nesting depth                 | Explicitly defined via ComponentId string references                  |
| **LLM Generation Reliability**  | Prone to syntax errors, missing brackets on large trees       | Highly efficient; components generated sequentially and independently |
| **Progressive Rendering**       | Difficult; parent container must fully close before rendering | Native; placeholders render dynamically based on ID references        |
| **Data Binding Implementation** | Hardcoded values deeply embedded in structure                 | Clean separation via precise JSON Pointer paths (e.g., /user/email)   |

This flat structure is exceptionally optimized for the token-by-token generation
of LLMs. The model can generate components sequentially, emitting the complete
definitions for text fields, buttons, and layout containers as entirely
independent objects within the flat array.23 Crucially, if an LLM generates a
parent component referencing a child ID that has not yet streamed down the SSE
connection, the A2UI client renderer is specifically designed to gracefully
handle this incomplete state by rendering visual placeholders, achieving true,
highly responsive, real-time progressive rendering for the end user.9

![][image3]

## **Core Message Types in A2UI**

The A2UI specification enforces a strict, uncompromising separation between the
structural layout of the user interface (the components) and the application
state (the data model) bound to it.20 This architectural separation is
facilitated through a sequence of specific, tightly defined JSON envelope
messages.23

The updateComponents (or surfaceUpdate in specific protocol versions) message is
exclusively responsible for delivering the UI component definitions.23 It
identifies the target visual canvas via a surfaceId and provides the flat
components array defining the layout via the adjacency list model.24 The
components defined within this layout specifically do not contain hardcoded data
values. Instead, input fields, textual displays, and interactive elements
utilize path strings (JSON Pointers utilizing a DynamicString or DynamicNumber
schema) to explicitly bind themselves to a separate data model.24

The dataModelUpdate message manages this underlying application state.23 When an
agent needs to dynamically populate form values, update displayed metrics, or
clear inputs, it issues this message. It specifies the target surfaceId, the
specific JSON Pointer path (e.g., /user/email or /flight/status), and the new
value.24

This strict architectural separation ensures that data can be updated
dynamically, instantly, and repeatedly as background tasks progress, without
requiring the agent to clumsily regenerate the entire UI structural hierarchy
and waste valuable, expensive LLM tokens.23 Once the requisite components are
delivered and the initial data model is fully populated, the agent transmits a
final beginRendering message, explicitly signaling the client to execute the
display routines and present the interface to the user.22

## **Encapsulating A2UI Payloads within the A2A Protocol**

While the A2UI standard brilliantly defines the structural semantics of the user
interface, it is fundamentally and intentionally transport-agnostic.23 The JSON
payloads can theoretically traverse a network via Server-Sent Events, standard
WebSockets, REST APIs, or custom proprietary event buses.23 However, in highly
distributed agentic architectures, A2UI operates symbiotically with the A2A
protocol through a strictly defined, standardized transport binding.23

The A2A extension ensures that A2UI messages automatically inherit A2A's robust
security, precise discovery mechanisms, and complex routing capabilities.23 When
developers elect to integrate A2UI over an A2A transport layer, rigorous
adherence to specific encapsulation and negotiation protocols is absolutely
mandatory to ensure reliable communication.

## **Extension Activation and Capability Negotiation**

Because not all A2A servers are equipped to generate UIs, and not all A2A
clients are sophisticated enough to support rich UI rendering, the A2UI
extension must be explicitly negotiated before transmission begins.25 For
JSON-RPC operations executed over HTTP transports, the client initiates this
activation phase by including an X-A2A-Extensions HTTP header containing the
exact, standardized URI corresponding to the supported A2UI version (e.g.,
https://a2ui.org/a2a-extension/a2ui/v0.8).25 This explicit header activation
acts as a signal to the remote agent that the client is fully equipped to parse
A2UI-specific events, understands the adjacency list model, and will correctly
format and dispatch user interaction payloads backward.25

Successful rendering also requires the generative agent to understand precisely
which UI components the host application is actually capable of displaying.20
Different host applications may possess wildly distinct component catalogs—a
rich mobile application might support native, complex date pickers and biometric
prompts, while a simple command-line interface or text-based chat window might
only support basic text rendering.8

To facilitate this vital synchronization, the client application packages an
a2uiClientCapabilities object directly into the metadata field of every single
A2A message it transmits to the server.25 This object explicitly lists the
supported catalogs (such as the standard catalog or custom enterprise variants)
and the exact component types available, guaranteeing that the generative agent
only selects and commands UI elements that are pre-approved and physically
renderable by the client's local security policies and frontend framework.9

## **Message Mapping and DataPart Encapsulation**

Once the extension is successfully active and operational capabilities are
synchronized, the server begins transmitting the A2UI JSON payload. Under the
strict rules of the A2A specification, each discrete A2UI envelope (such as a
distinct updateComponents layout object or a dataModelUpdate state object) must
be precisely mapped to a single A2A message Part.24

To properly accommodate structured JSON data, the A2A protocol utilizes the
versatile DataPart schema.11 However, a significant routing problem arises: how
does the client distinguish standard structured data meant for algorithmic
processing from executable A2UI instructions meant for rendering? To solve this,
the DataPart must be wrapped with highly specific typing metadata.25

The metadata dictionary embedded within the specific DataPart must include a
mimeType key strictly and exclusively assigned the string value
application/json+a2ui.25 Only when this specific MIME type is detected will the
A2A client's routing logic divert the contents of the data field to the
specialized A2UI rendering engine, ensuring the UI is built rather than
displaying the raw, underlying JSON array as text to the bewildered end user.21

## **Managing Interactivity: The Read/Write Contract and Data Synchronization**

A rich user interface is inherently bidirectional. Users interact with the
rendered components—typing text, clicking toggles, adjusting sliders—and those
actions must be securely, accurately routed back to the controlling agent to
inform its subsequent operations.9 A2UI over A2A governs this critical
interactivity through a precise read/write contract and stringent metadata
management designed specifically to prevent state corruption and catastrophic
data leakage across multiple agents operating on the same screen.28

## **The Client-to-Server Read/Write Contract**

The A2UI protocol dictates an uncompromising rule: the client application serves
as the absolute, authoritative source of truth for the local application state
(the Data Model) during the entire duration of user interaction.28 When a user
modifies an input component—such as typing an address into a TextField or
selecting a boolean in a CheckBox—the client immediately executes a synchronous
write operation, committing the new value directly to the corresponding path in
the local data model.28 There is no round-trip delay to the server to validate
keystrokes.

When the user eventually triggers an actionable component intended to submit
data (like clicking a primary "Submit" or "Confirm" button), the client
constructs an action payload strictly conforming to the client-to-server JSON
schema.25 Rather than lazily sending the entire application state back to the
agent, the action payload intelligently isolates a "view" or a highly specific
subset of the data model relevant only to that specific event.28 The client
programmatically resolves all requisite path references against its
synchronously updated local data model, packages the specific values required by
the agent into the action payload, and dispatches it across the A2A transport as
a standard incoming message.28 This ensures bandwidth efficiency and prevents
the remote agent from having to parse irrelevant state data.

## **Metadata Stripping and the Surface Ownership Pattern**

In sophisticated, enterprise-grade multi-agent environments, a central
"orchestrator" client often interfaces directly with the human user, while
concurrently delegating disparate, specialized UI elements to various background
sub-agents.28 For example, a financial orchestrator might display a unified
dashboard where a highly secure banking sub-agent renders an account balance
widget in one surface, while a less secure, third-party weather sub-agent
renders a local forecast in another surface on the exact same screen.28

To maintain total state synchronization without overwhelming the transport
layer, the client often attaches the complete application state (the global
a2uiClientDataModel object) to the metadata field of its outgoing A2A envelopes
rather than generating independent, discrete synchronization messages.24 While
highly efficient for bandwidth, this introduces a critical, potentially
devastating security vector known as "State Scraping".28 If the orchestrator
carelessly forwards the entire envelope to a delegated sub-agent, a compromised
or malicious sub-agent (like the weather app) could extract the global
a2uiClientDataModel from the metadata and illicitly read sensitive data managed
by other agents (like the banking details) on the same screen.28

The protocol mandates mitigation of this threat through obligatory metadata
stripping and the implementation of the Surface Ownership Pattern.28 When a
sub-agent requests the creation of a UI surface via a createSurface message, the
orchestrator intercepts the request and logs an immutable mapping linking the
newly generated surfaceId specifically to that sub-agent's identity.28 When user
action payloads return from the client, the orchestrator consults this mapping,
identifies the responsible sub-agent, ruthlessly strips all global data models
from the metadata, and programmatically routes only the isolated action request
to the target.28 This ensures that enterprise trust boundaries remain intact,
and sub-agents remain entirely opaque and blind to the operations and data of
their peers.4

## **Implementation Logistics: Generating A2UI via Large Language Models**

Adhering to the exacting specifications of A2A and A2UI requires distinct,
highly engineered adjustments to the prompting infrastructure governing the
backend Large Language Models. Because A2UI relies entirely on generative output
to construct interfaces on the fly, the LLM must be explicitly, exhaustively
instructed on how to format its response to satisfy both human readability and
the rigid programmatic parsing engines listening on the A2A server.30

The standard implementation involves prepending comprehensive system
instructions, strict behavioral rules, and the complete A2UI_SCHEMA directly
into the agent's active context window.30 The prompt mandates strict adherence
to a bipartite output structure, enforcing the use of a predefined, highly
specific delimiter, specifically \---a2ui_JSON---.30

The LLM is explicitly instructed to generate its conversational, human-readable
text response first.30 Following the completion of the text, it must inject the
\---a2ui_JSON--- delimiter on a new line.30 Immediately below the delimiter, the
model must transition from natural language generation to strict code
generation, outputting the raw JSON array representing the A2UI message payload
(such as the updateComponents array utilizing the adjacency list model).30

By rigorously standardizing this bipartite output pattern, the backend parsing
logic is vastly simplified and highly performant.30 The A2A server application
intercepts the raw output stream from the LLM, splits the string array upon
detecting the \---a2ui_JSON--- delimiter, and processes the two halves entirely
independently.16 The conversational text is mapped to a standard A2A TextPart,
while the JSON array is validated against the local schema, enclosed securely
within an A2A DataPart tagged with the application/json+a2ui MIME type, and
finally transmitted over the network.16 This workflow maximizes token
efficiency, prevents hallucinated schema elements, and ensures that the
rendering client receives perfectly formatted, transport-compliant payloads.22

## **Security, Authentication, and Authorization Frameworks**

As the A2A protocol facilitates collaboration across disparate cloud
environments, vendor platforms, and organizational trust boundaries, it
necessitates robust, enterprise-grade security controls. These controls are not
bolted on as an afterthought but are embedded directly into the transport,
identity verification, and task assignment processes from the ground up.2
Security within A2A is treated as an integral component of the protocol's
fundamental architecture.2

At the most foundational layer, all communication within the A2A ecosystem is
strictly mandated to occur over HTTP(S). This enforces standard Transport Layer
Security (TLS) encryption to guarantee absolute data privacy and payload
integrity in transit, defending against man-in-the-middle attacks.4 The
discovery mechanism via the Agent Card provides the initial layer of operational
trust. It allows clients to verify the publicly stated capabilities, required
cryptographic schemas, and endpoint configurations of a remote agent before ever
initiating data exchange.10

Authentication within A2A is multifaceted and highly context-dependent, designed
to support a myriad of enterprise configurations. A2A natively supports the
utilization of JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for secure identity assertion.12 A remote
A2A Server can authenticate itself to a client—such as when establishing a
webhook for asynchronous Push Notifications—by generating a JWT signed
cryptographically with its own private key.12 The server subsequently exposes
its corresponding public keys via a standardized JSON Web Key Set (JWKS)
endpoint.12 Upon receiving a notification payload, the client interrogates the
JWT headers, retrieves the public key from the advertised JWKS endpoint, and
cryptographically verifies the signature and claims (such as issuer, audience,
and expiration) to definitively guarantee the authenticity of the sender.12

The protocol further accommodates highly complex enterprise environments through
a mechanism known as In-Task Authentication, allowing for secondary credential
management during long-running workflows.33 If a delegated task requires the
remote agent to access a protected internal database or a secure third-party
API, the agent transitions the task status to an auth-required state.33 It then
emits a TaskStatus update detailing the necessary authorization mechanisms to
the client.33 The orchestrating client is then responsible for executing the
required OAuth flows, procuring the secondary credentials out-of-band, and
supplying them to the agent via a subsequent message/send operation to resume
processing.33

This design ensures that sensitive credential handling is isolated and strictly
managed. However, enterprise system architects must remain vigilant. Security
analyses of early A2A implementations highlight that architects must carefully
manage risks such as coarse-grained scopes or excessive token lifetimes when
designing multi-agent topologies.2 Because A2A inherently enables agents to
forward data to other agents, preventing unauthorized data exfiltration requires
strict adherence to token minimization and the implementation of robust consent
mechanisms before sensitive data is shared across trust boundaries.2

## **Ecosystem Context: A2A, MCP, and AG-UI**

Understanding the A2A protocol requires placing it within the broader context of
the 2026 Agent Protocol Stack. A2A does not operate in a vacuum; it functions
synergistically with other specialized protocols to construct a complete,
end-to-end framework for artificial intelligence operations.12

While the Model Context Protocol (MCP) serves to standardize how a single agent
connects "downward" to internal databases, file systems, and specific toolsets,
A2A acts as the "lateral" communication framework.6 MCP treats external systems
as tools for an agent to utilize.29 Conversely, A2A treats other agents as
intelligent peers to collaborate with.29 For example, a retail orchestrator
agent might use A2A to securely delegate a supply chain query to an inventory
specialist agent. That specialized inventory agent would then utilize MCP to
actually query the SQL database and retrieve the stock levels.7 The protocols
are not competing; they are perfectly complementary at different layers of the
architectural stack.29

Furthermore, the Agent-User Interaction Protocol (AG-UI) acts as a runtime
interaction protocol and event bus.22 While A2UI defines _what_ the UI should
look like and mean (the declarative JSON payload), AG-UI defines _how_ the agent
and UI talk to each other in real-time, utilizing delta-based, event-driven
architecture rather than sending snapshot re-renders.22 A complete, modern
application might utilize AG-UI to handle the bidirectional state
synchronization and chat history, utilizing A2UI payloads to define the
generative interfaces, sending tasks laterally to peers via A2A, and querying
data downward via MCP.20

The primary architectural challenge remaining in this ecosystem is
observability.29 Tracing a user request that flows from a frontend A2UI
interaction, through an AG-UI transport layer, laterally across an A2A task
delegation, and downward into an MCP tool execution requires highly
sophisticated telemetry capable of stitching together distinct error models
across multiple distributed systems.29

## **Conclusion**

The Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol represents a critical maturation point in the
deployment of artificial intelligence. By providing a rigid, standardized
communication layer, A2A eliminates the bespoke integration silos that
previously constrained multi-agent collaboration.1 Through strict adherence to
the JSON-RPC 2.0 format, the protocol ensures highly predictable, method-driven
interactions suitable for autonomous agents.4 Furthermore, its robust support
for Server-Sent Events guarantees that even the most complex, long-running
generative tasks can stream real-time updates and chunked artifacts back to
clients seamlessly.12

When paired with the Agent-to-User Interface (A2UI) extension, the A2A protocol
expands its utility from pure backend coordination to comprehensive, secure
frontend generation.8 By standardizing how interface definitions are
encapsulated within DataPart payloads and leveraging the LLM-friendly Adjacency
List Model, A2UI ensures that intelligent agents can dynamically construct
multimodal experiences that are secure, strictly branded, and natively
rendered.9 As the industry continues to pivot toward distributed agentic
networks, mastering the mechanics, chunking algorithms, and security paradigms
of the A2A protocol is an absolute necessity for engineering robust,
interoperable AI architectures.
